---
description: "ef core migrations must be generated via `dotnet ef migrations add` — never hand-written, breaks model snapshot"
globs: ["**/Migrations/**/*.cs", "**/Migrations/**/*.Designer.cs"]
always: false
---

# EF Core migrations — tool-generated only

## Rule

**All EF Core migration files in `**/Migrations/**/*.cs` and `**/Migrations/**/*.Designer.cs` are generated by `dotnet ef migrations add` / `dotnet ef migrations remove` (and the matching snapshot files by `dotnet ef migrations script` / scaffold commands).**

They **must never** be:

- Hand-written (even partial edits to `Up()` / `Down()` bodies)
- Hand-edited after generation (except for the rare cases documented in `.agents/rules/coding/ef-core.md` §9 — custom SQL EF can't express, and only with a justifying comment)
- Copy-pasted from another project or branch
- Generated by a different tool or scaffold than the one the project uses (`dotnet ef` for EF Core)

## Why

EF Core migrations are tightly coupled to the **model snapshot** (the `.Designer.cs` and `*ModelSnapshot.cs` files). The snapshot is the source of truth that `dotnet ef migrations add` diffs against to produce the next migration's `Up()`/`Down()`. If the snapshot is out of sync with the actual DbContext model:

- `dotnet ef migrations add NextName` produces a migration that re-emits schema already in the snapshot → duplicate columns / tables in production
- `dotnet ef migrations script` produces drift between what EF thinks the schema is and what's actually in the database
- Down-migrations (`migrations remove`) fail with `The migration 'X' is in the snapshot but not in the database` because the snapshot diverged from the live schema

The `.Designer.cs` file is generated code — never touch it. The `*ModelSnapshot.cs` file is regenerated automatically by `dotnet ef` on every migration add/remove.

## How to recover when this rule was violated

If migration files were hand-written or hand-edited (i.e. already in the repo before this rule existed):

1. `git rm` the offending `Migrations/**/*.cs` and `Migrations/**/*.Designer.cs` files for that module
2. Delete the matching `*ModelSnapshot.cs`
3. Ensure the DbContext is in the **original** state (the state before the bad migration was committed)
4. Run `dotnet ef migrations add InitialSchema --project <Module>.Infrastructure --startup-project <Migrator or Host> --context <Module>DbContext` to regenerate against the current model
5. Review the regenerated files via `git diff` — they should match what the DbContext currently produces
6. Apply the migration to a fresh database and verify the schema matches what the application expects

For a brand-new module that has never had a real migration: start with `dotnet ef migrations add InitialSchema`, never with a hand-written migration.

## Verification

```bash
# Find any Migrations files that were modified by anything other than dotnet ef:
git log --diff-filter=M --name-only -- 'src/**/Migrations/**/*.cs' 'src/**/Migrations/**/*.Designer.cs' \
    | while read f; do
        commit=$(git log -1 --format='%H' -- "$f")
        author=$(git log -1 --format='%an' -- "$f")
        msg=$(git log -1 --format='%s' -- "$f")
        echo "$f | $author | $msg"
    done
```

Any commit touching `Migrations/**/*.cs` or `Migrations/**/*.Designer.cs` should reference `dotnet ef migrations add/remove/script` in the message or be the result of running the recovery procedure above.

## Source-gen treatment — fully handled, no wrapper needed

EF migrations are tool-generated, but `dotnet ef migrations add` does NOT emit
the `[GeneratedCode]` attribute manually in the source files. EF Core 9+ runs a
Roslyn **source generator** that synthesizes the attribute at compile time
(see `Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design`). Additionally, the tool emits
`// <auto-generated />` as the first line of each Designer + Snapshot file.
Together these are sufficient for every downstream tool:

- **`dotnet format`**: recognizes the `// <auto-generated />` comment marker
  AND skips files matching the `[**/Migrations/**]`, `[**/Migrations/*.cs]`
  editorconfig severity overrides. The format-check gate in
  `src/build/Plexor.Build.Tools/Plexor.Build.Tools.targets` additionally
  passes `--exclude "**/Migrations/**.cs"` (along with `*.Designer.cs`,
  `*ModelSnapshot.cs`) to skip migrations from both formatting AND
  verification.
- **Roslyn analyzers** (CA / RCS / MA / IDE): see the severity overrides in
  `.editorconfig`'s `[**/Migrations/**]` block. The IDs that still fire
  at `suggestion` / `hidden` severity (naming convention `IDE1006`,
  lambda-as-static `IDE0320`, named-argument order `RCS1205`, etc.) are
  Roslynator IDE-style suggestions, not errors — they appear in the
  format-check output but are excluded by the `dotnet format` `--exclude`
  path filter and don't trip the build.

**Don't try to inject `[GeneratedCode]` manually.** EF's source generator
already produces one, and a second `[GeneratedCode]` on the same assembly
trips `error CS0579: Duplicate attribute "GeneratedCode"`. If you're tempted
to "improve" the generated files — don't.

**Don't try to override the `[**/Migrations/**]` editorconfig block with
more `none` severities.** The format-check `--exclude` is the right place
to silence migration noise. Editorconfig is for human-authored code;
the more rules you disable for migrations, the more exceptions you create
for future analyzer additions that need to fire in human code.

## Workflow

```bash
# Create a new migration. EF tool emits Designer + Snapshot + body
# files automatically; the build-tools target excludes them from the
# format-check gate.
bash -c "\
  export MIGRATOR_CONNECTION='Host=localhost;Port=47100;Database=plexor;Username=plexor;Password=plexor' && \
  dotnet ef migrations add ClusterRuntimeId_Add \
    --project src/modules/Plexor.Modules.Clusters/Plexor.Modules.Clusters.Infrastructure \
    --startup-project src/host/Plexor.Migrator \
    --context ClusterDbContext \
    -o Migrations/Clusters"

# Apply migrations to a fresh database (during deploy or first-run).
dotnet run --project src/host/Plexor.Migrator

# (Or per-context if the all-in-one migrator is misbehaving -- see
# `tools/migrate.sh` for a workaround.)
```

## Squashing migrations

When the migration history gets messy (multiple small migrations that
should have been one), **delete all old migration files** and let
`dotnet ef migrations add` regenerate a single squashed migration
against the current DbContext model. Plexor is v0.1 pre-release with no
production data — squashing is safe.

```bash
# 1. Drop the dev database (or psql the module schemas).
podman exec plexor-postgres psql -U plexor -d plexor -c \
  "DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS forge CASCADE;
   DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS sigil CASCADE;
   DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS realm CASCADE;
   DROP TABLE IF EXISTS \"__EFMigrationsHistory\";"

# 2. Delete the migration folders from disk.
find src -path "*/Migrations/*.cs" -not -path "*/bin/*" -not -path "*/obj/*" -delete
find src -type d -name Migrations -empty -delete

# 3. Regenerate a single squashed migration per DbContext.
dotnet ef migrations add _Initial \
    --project src/modules/Plexor.Modules.Clusters/Plexor.Modules.Clusters.Infrastructure \
    --startup-project src/host/Plexor.Migrator \
    --context ClusterDbContext \
    -o Migrations/Clusters
# (repeat for RealmDbContext, IdentityDbContext, RevokedCertsDbContext)

# 4. Apply.
dotnet run --project src/host/Plexor.Migrator
```

The squashed migration has the same model snapshot as the chain of
incremental migrations it replaced, so production databases rebuilt
from scratch arrive at the same schema.
